coredata.dc:title: Augmenting long range SPR sensitivity with quantum dots-AgNP-PVA layer for methylene blue detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bakar, Mohd Hafiz Abu
Othman, Muhammad Qayyum
Azeman, Nur Hidayah
Mobarak, Nadhratun Naiim
Mohd Daniyal, Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim
Khushaini, Muhammad Asif Ahmad
Apsari, Retna
Abdullah, Fairuz
A. Bakar, Ahmad Ashrif
coredata.dc:description: This study introduces an innovative Long-Range Surface Plasmon Resonance (LRSPR) technique, incorporating the silver nanoparticle-amine functionalized carbon quantum dot-polyvinyl alcohol (AgNP-NCQD-PVA) layer as both a dielectric buffer layer and a sensing material. This integration substantially enhances the sensitivity of LRSPR for methylene blue detection, marking a significant advancement in photonic technology. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR demonstrates exceptional sensitivity with two distinct slopes: 4.2473 nm/ppm for the range of 0.5–5 ppm (R²=0.9231) and 0.5279 nm/ppm for the range of 5–50 ppm (R²=0.9629). These results indicate improved accuracy and reliability over bare Au LRSPR, which offers a more limited detection range of 0.5–5 ppm. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR has a higher resolution than bare Au-LRSPR, as indicated by its FOM values of 0.0332 ppm⁻¹ . The higher binding affinity (1.642 × 104 M−1) of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR further enhances its performance, making it a more effective sensor for detecting minute changes in analyte concentration. These innovations set new standards in sensor performance, promising significant advancements in high-precision photonic sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Surface Plasmon Resonance
Quantum Dots
Nanoparticles
Polymer
And Binding Affinity

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PVA-AgNP-NCQD LRSPR glass substrate
Conventional cSPR and LRSPR for methylene detection
LRSPR utilizing AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer as sensing material
Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author acknowledges the Research University Grant (GUP), grant number GUP-2023-043, funded by the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Malaysia.

The authors would like to thank the Photonics Laboratory of the Department of Electrical, Electronic & Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Faculty of Science and Technology, and the Centre for Research and Instrument Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) for all amenities provided. Also, this work was supported by Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and UNITEN through the BOLD Refresh Postdoctoral Fellowships under Grant J510050002-IC-6 BOLDREFRESH2025-Centre of Excellence.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram and actual configuration of LRSPR setup using Kretschmann configuration.

Spectra of SPR for (a) the full spectrum showing peaks for both conventional SPR and LRSPR and (b) a zoomed-in subset of the spectrum highlighting the peak for conventional SPR, measured for different concentrations of methylene blue ranging from 0 to 1000 ppm.
Standard curve of SPR wavelength shifting against different concentrations of methylene blue for cSPR and LRSPR.
Spectrum of LRSPR coated with AgNP-NCQD-PVA for different methylene blue concentrations ranging from 0 (DI) to 1000 ppm.
Standard curve of LRSPR for methylene blue within the range of (a) 0.5 – 1000 ppm and (b) 0.5 – 50 ppm.
Sips isotherm model of the SPR shift for methylene blue using AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer (symmetric LRSPR) and bare Au (anti-symmetric LRSPR).
Resonance wavelength of Au and Au-AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR for deionized water (Ref) and different concentrations of methylene blue.
Performance comparison of bare Au LRSPR, AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR, and other reported techniques for the detection of methylene blue.

head.ce:title: Augmenting long range SPR sensitivity with quantum dots-AgNP-PVA layer for methylene blue detection

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Photonics Technology Laboratory, Department of Electrical, Electronic and Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study introduces an innovative Long-Range Surface Plasmon Resonance (LRSPR) technique, incorporating the silver nanoparticle-amine functionalized carbon quantum dot-polyvinyl alcohol (AgNP-NCQD-PVA) layer as both a dielectric buffer layer and a sensing material. This integration substantially enhances the sensitivity of LRSPR for methylene blue detection, marking a significant advancement in photonic technology. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR demonstrates exceptional sensitivity with two distinct slopes: 4.2473 nm/ppm for the range of 0.5–5 ppm (R²=0.9231) and 0.5279 nm/ppm for the range of 5–50 ppm (R²=0.9629). These results indicate improved accuracy and reliability over bare Au LRSPR, which offers a more limited detection range of 0.5–5 ppm. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR has a higher resolution than bare Au-LRSPR, as indicated by its FOM values of 0.0332 ppm⁻¹ . The higher binding affinity (1.642 × 104 M−1) of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR further enhances its performance, making it a more effective sensor for detecting minute changes in analyte concentration. These innovations set new standards in sensor performance, promising significant advancements in high-precision photonic sensing applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Detecting methylene blue (MB) dye is vital due to its widespread industrial use and the environmental and health risks it poses. Industrial discharge of MB can damage aquatic ecosystems, persist in water sources, and contribute to health issues such as methemoglobinemia in humans. Prolonged exposure heightens these risks, underscoring the need for effective detection and quantification in environmental monitoring [1,2]. A few sensing technologies offer a reliable alternative for detecting MB in water at very low concentrations, enabling timely intervention. Among various methods, including high-performance liquid chromatography [3], electrochemical techniques [4], fiber optic sensor [5], and Raman scattering [6,7], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors stand out for their high sensitivity, compact design, and portability.

SPR has revolutionized sensor applications by detecting minute refractive index changes through surface plasmon excitation at a metal-dielectric interface. Conventional SPR (cSPR) setups employ a thin plasmonic layer, typically gold or silver, between a prism and sensing layer, where p-polarized light generates a resonance dip in the reflected spectrum. Changes in the refractive index shift this dip, enabling precise detection [8–10]. However, cSPR’s short plasmon propagation length, due to intrinsic damping, limits sensitivity and accuracy in precise applications [11]. Addressing these limitations, Long-Range Surface Plasmon Resonance (LRSPR) integrates a dielectric buffer layer (DBL) between the substrate and metal film, extending plasmon propagation length. This design weakens plasmon wave confinement, enhancing analyte interaction through deeper penetration [12–14]. LRSPR achieves superior electromagnetic field intensity, narrower full width at half maximum (FWHM), and higher sensitivity, making it ideal for advanced healthcare, environmental monitoring, and industrial sensing applications [15–19].

This study introduces a novel AgNP-NCQD-PVA composite layer for LRSPR sensors, offering dual functionality: optical enhancement and analyte interaction. Silver nanoparticles boost the local electromagnetic field, enhancing refractive index sensitivity. At the same time, carbon quantum dots improve detection by facilitating chemical interactions with methylene blue and acting as fluorophores to increase signal sensitivity [20,21]. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) provides structural stability and serves as an additional dielectric buffer layer (DBL), enhancing plasmonic symmetry for better signal resolution [22,23]. This innovative integration of AgNP, NCQD, and PVA into the LRSPR system enables simultaneous enhancement of optical performance and analyte interaction. This demonstrates the significant potential for advanced sensing applications, particularly in detecting pollutants like methylene blue.

By leveraging the dual-functional AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer, this study aims to demonstrate good sensitivity and detection range in methylene blue detection, while advancing the capabilities of LRSPR technology. The proposed dual function of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer represents a breakthrough in sensor technology. This study offers new insights into integrating multifunctional materials into LRSPR systems.

Polyethyleneimine, citric acid, isopropyl alcohol, sodium chloride, acetone, polyvinyl alcohol, silver nitrate (AgNO3), trisodium citrate, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and sodium borohydride (NaBH4) were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. R&M Chemicals supplied metal salts, ethylenediamine, and 30 % ammonia solution.

The synthesis of AgNP followed the established protocol outlined in the references [24,25]. Initially, 0.2 mL of a 0.05 M AgNO3 solution was introduced into 96.56 mL of deionized water, followed by stirring at 900 rpm. Subsequently, 2.0 mL of 75 mM trisodium citrate and 0.24 mL of 30 % H2O2 were added to the mixture. Immediately afterward, 1.0 mL of NaBH4 was swiftly injected. The mixture was observed until the formation of a dark blue color indicative of AgNP solution. Finally, the resulting solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 1 h to isolate the dark blue sediment, yielding approximately 2 mL of the sediment.

The synthesis of functionalized CQD involved a two-step hydrothermal process following a previously documented procedure [21]. Initially, citric acid (1.0507 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, and 335 microliters of ethylenediamine were added to the solution, which was then heated at 200 °C for 12 h in a Teflon-lined autoclave. After the reaction, the CQD solution was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. Subsequently, 1 mL of polyethyleneimine solution was introduced, and the resulting mixture was heated for an additional 5 h at 80 °C. The solution was then subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22-μm membrane filter. Finally, the supernatant solution was collected and dialyzed using a dialysis bag for a day, yielding the NCQD solution for further characterization.

The Au film was fabricated using a well-established and optimized procedure to achieve a thickness of 50 nm [9]. A glass substrate (24 mm × 24 mm, 0.1 mm thick) was purchased from Menzel Glaser, cleaned, and then coated with the gold layer using a K575X sputter coater (Quorum Technologies, West Sussex, UK). The sputtering process was performed for 67 s, with a gas pressure of 5 psi and a 25 mm distance between the substrate and the target, resulting in a gold thickness of 50 nm. Following the coating, the substrate was cleaned with ethanol before being utilized in the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA-AgNP-NCQD LRSPR glass substrate
ce:section.ce:para: Two vials, denoted as A and B to represent embedded and unembedded with PVA polymer respectively, were utilized for the experiment. In vial A, 0.5 g of PVA was diluted in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water at 70 °C for 3 h until fully dissolved, while vial B received 10 mL of DI water. Next, 1 mL of synthesized NCQD was added to both vials at room temperature and mixed thoroughly until homogenous. Subsequently, 2 mL of blue AgNP sediment was diluted to 10 mL using DI water, and 6 mL of this mixture was added to both vials A and B, followed by thorough mixing until homogeneity was achieved. Finally, a 0.1 mL aliquot of the resulting composites (A and B, respectively) was applied onto a prepared Au glass substrate using a spin coater set at 1500 rpm, and the coated slide was dried in an oven at 50 °C for 10 min.

In this study, LRSPR measurements were conducted using lab-scale LRSPR spectroscopy with the Kretschmann configuration, as shown in Fig. 1. The experimental setup featured a broadband light source (200 nm to 1100 nm) from Ocean Optic light (DH-2000-BAL). Light transmission in the system was facilitated by a multimode fiber optic SMA-SMA patch cord with a 400 μm core diameter. The light was then polarized using a polarizer (400 nm – 1100 nm) to illuminate only p-polarized light, with an iris used to control the light spot size. The p-polarized light required for LRSPR excitation was directed at the SF11 prism at a fixed incidence angle of 53.8°, which was determined through theoretical simulations. Fig. S1 shows the angular SPR response obtained from these simulations, performed using SF11 (n = 1.77861) and a 650 nm wavelength with deionized water as the reference medium. The refractive index of gold in the simulations was modeled using data from Johnson and Christy [26]. These simulations were used to determine the initial incidence angle for this experiment. The reflected beam was captured by a photodetector using an Ocean Optic spectrometer (HR4000CG-UV-NIR). Spectra recorded by the fiber optic spectrometer were analyzed with SpectraSuite software.

For each concentration, the reference spectrum (air spectrum without analyte) was obtained before starting the measurement. The sample was then introduced, and the corresponding LRSPR peak was recorded. Afterward, the sample was removed, and the LRSPR chip was rinsed with distilled water and dried to restore the reference spectrum before proceeding to the next concentration. Analytes were prepared from standard methylene blue solutions (1000 ppm), with 9 different concentrations ranging from 0.5 ppm to 1000 ppm, achieved through dilution with deionized water (DI). The DI used had a resistivity of 18 megohm-cm to minimize potential interference from other species during the measurement process. This experiment was conducted at room temperature under controlled conditions to ensure consistent readings, and the procedure was repeated five times for each methylene blue concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated by assessing the standard deviation (SY) of the intercept and the slope (S) of the calibration curve. It represents the concentration at which the technique’s response reaches 3.3(SY/S).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conventional cSPR and LRSPR for methylene detection
ce:section.ce:para: In SPR studies, the interaction of light with a metal-dielectric interface generates resonance peaks that reveal critical details about various sensing configurations. When light is incident at the optimal angle in an LRSPR configuration, both surface plasmon modes associated with LRSPR and cSPR can be detected within the spectrum. As shown in Fig. 2, two distinct resonance peaks appear: the first, a less intense peak, corresponds to short-range SPR (often referred to as cSPR), while the second, more pronounced peak represents LRSPR. This dual-resonance behavior, evident in the spectrum, highlights the unique characteristics of LRSPR, a phenomenon previously reported in other studies using an SF11 prism, fiber optic SPR configurations, and simulations [27–29]. These two SPR dips arise from the excitation of surface plasmons at two distinct interfaces: one at the Au-dielectric buffer layer interface and the other at the Au-water (medium) interface. In Fig. 2(a), both peaks are shown across the full spectral range, clearly illustrating the separation between cSPR and LRSPR for different concentrations of methylene blue ranging from 0 to 1000 ppm. Meanwhile, Fig. 2(b) provides a magnified view of the cSPR peak at 437 nm, which is characterized by weaker light coupling due to increased damping and lower reflectance efficiency. In contrast, the LRSPR peak at 680 nm demonstrates superior performance, with reduced losses, extended propagation, and more efficient light coupling, resulting in a significantly sharper and more intense resonance [18,27]. However, as the concentration of Methylene Blue increases, the LRSPR peak becomes broader, and its intensity diminishes. This occurs because methylene blue absorbs light in the same wavelength range as the LRSPR resonance [30]. At higher concentrations, this absorption increases, reducing the amount of light available to effectively excite the surface plasmons. Consequently, the energy transfer between the incident light and the plasmons becomes less efficient, causing damping of the plasmonic oscillations.

Fig. 3 further analyzes these observations by comparing the SPR wavelength shifts for cSPR and LRSPR against various concentrations of methylene blue. As shown in Fig. 3, cSPR exhibits minimal wavelength shifting (∼2 nm) and a plateau trend with increasing methylene blue concentration. In contrast, LRSPR shows a substantial wavelength shift (∼50 nm) and a continuously growing trend. This comparison highlights the superior sensitivity and resonance intensity of LRSPR over cSPR due to the properties of SPW of LRSPR that have a deeper penetration depth compared to the cSPR, demonstrating LRSPR's greater effectiveness in detecting subtle analyte changes, such as methylene blue in solution [29,31]. The LRPSR has a greater penetration depth due to enhanced plasmon interactions and additional layers, which allows for more extensive interaction with the surrounding medium [12,16]. Consequently, this configuration exhibits a more gradual saturation as the concentration of methylene blue increases.

ce:section.ce:section-title: LRSPR utilizing AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer as sensing material
ce:section.ce:para: The FESEM image of the Au glass substrate coated with the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer, shown in Fig. S2(a), illustrates that the layer exhibits a uniform distribution across the surface, reflecting consistent coverage and effective fabrication methods. The surface morphology is characterized by granular textures and nanoscale roughness, which arise from the embedded silver nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots within the PVA matrix. This nanoscale roughness enhances the functional properties of the layer by increasing the surface area available for interactions. The observed cracks and wrinkles in the layer are attributed to the intermolecular forces within the composite material, which, combined with the drying and curing processes during fabrication, induce internal stresses that lead to the formation of cracks and wrinkles on the surface [32]. Meanwhile, Figs S2(b) and S2(c) present the TEM analysis of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer. Fig. S2(b) shows triangular-shaped AgNP with an average size of 55.46 nm, confirming their successful synthesis. Fig. S2(c) displays the TEM image of NCQD with an average size of 4.52 nm, indicating the successful fabrication of the quantum dots. The morphology of the synthesized AgNP and NCQD aligns with previously reported research [21,24,25]. However, due to the differences in electron density between AgNP and NCQD, the TEM analysis cannot simultaneously capture both structures in a single image, demonstrating a limitation of the technique in visualizing composite materials [33].

Fig. 4 illustrates the broad light spectrum ranging from 400 nm to 1000 nm passing through the LRSPR setup, utilizing the fabricated AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer as the sensing layer, measured for methylene blue concentrations ranging from 0 to 1000 ppm. The LRSPR fingerprint peak is distinctly observed within this range, demonstrating strong resonance between 640 nm and 760 nm. This peak, attributed to the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer coated on the Au substrate, effectively inducing LRSPR. The LRSPR peak for the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer appears at 696 nm in deionized water (DI) and shifts to higher wavelengths as the methylene blue concentration increases. Fig. S3 presents the stability graph of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LSPR sensor in detecting varying concentrations of methylene blue. The graph illustrates that the AgNP-NCQD-PVA-based LSPR sensor maintains largely stable performance over time, with only minor fluctuations in the baseline. However, as seen in Fig. S3, a decrease in baseline intensity after multiple measurements can introduce a small error in the sensor’s performance. This is likely due to residual analyte remaining on the sensor surface, despite flushing with DI water [34].

Table 1 compares the resonance wavelengths and shifts between the bare Au LRSPR and the Au-AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR. Meanwhile, Fig. 5(a) further illustrates the correlation between wavelength shift and methylene blue concentration. The results indicate that the Au-AgNP-NCQD-PVA configuration exhibits a consistent red shift in resonance wavelength as the methylene blue concentration increases, demonstrating a strong linear response up to 50 ppm. According to the Fresnel equations, changes in methylene blue concentration affect its permittivity (ε
2
), which is directly linked to the refractive index. This influences the wave vector (k
x ), as described by the dispersion relation. The resulting change in k
x alters the reflectance, driving shifts in this LRSPR signal [35]. Beyond 50 ppm, the shift begins to diminish due to the saturation effect of the LRSPR sensor, where the plasmonic resonance approaches its sensitivity limit. At higher concentrations, this limit reduces the system's ability to detect further changes in the refractive index. In contrast, the bare Au configuration demonstrates a more modest redshift, with a detectable response only up to 5 ppm before reaching its saturation point.

Fig. 5(b) provides a detailed view of the standard curve illustrating the LRSPR shift as a function of methylene blue concentration, ranging from 0.5 to 50.0 ppm, used for performance analysis. Linear regression analysis reveals distinct sensitivity slopes for the two LRSPR configurations. In the bare Au LRSPR setup, the sensitivity within the 0.5–5.0 ppm range is 1.3655 nm/ppm, with a correlation coefficient (R²) of 0.3425. Beyond this range, from 5.0 to 50.0 ppm, the sensitivity decreases to 0.1008 nm/ppm, with an improved R² of 0.7341. Conversely, the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR configuration exhibits significantly higher sensitivities. In the 0.5–5.0 ppm range, the sensitivity is 4.2473 nm/ppm with a strong correlation (R² = 0.9231). For the 5.0–50.0 ppm range, the sensitivity remains higher than the bare Au, at 0.5279 nm/ppm, with an R² of 0.9629.

The differences in sensitivity between the two LRSPR configurations can be attributed to both penetration depth and chemical interactions with the sensing materials [12,16,36]. The addition of an extra layer of sensing material AgNP-NCQD-PVA has induced the symmetrical configuration of LRSPR that has the benefits of a greater penetration depth compared to the bare Au LRSPR [16,37]. This extended depth allows for a more extensive interaction with the surrounding medium, resulting in a more gradual saturation process and greater sensitivity, especially at lower concentrations. In addition, the sensing material in the Au-AgNP-NCQD-PVA setup further contributes to its enhanced performance. Functional groups on the NCQD, such as amino or carboxyl groups, and hydroxyl groups on PVA, can interact with methylene blue through hydrogen bonding, ionic interactions, or electrostatic attractions [21,38]. These interactions facilitate the accumulation of more methylene blue molecules within the penetration depth region, thereby improving sensitivity and extending the effective interaction volume. Meanwhile, the simpler bare Au configuration, with a lack of additional functional groups, limits its ability to attract and bind methylene blue, resulting in a more localized and less sensitive response.

The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR configuration outperforms the bare Au LRSPR by achieving a FOM of 0.0332 ppm⁻¹ for the 0.5–5 ppm range and 0.0041 ppm⁻¹ for the 5.0–50 ppm range, both of which are higher than the FOM of 0.0145 ppm⁻¹ and 0.0011 ppm⁻¹ for the bare Au LRSPR. This higher FOM directly correlates to better resolution, as resolution is inversely proportional to the FOM. Therefore, AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR not only offers enhanced sensitivity but also superior resolution, allowing for the detection of smaller changes in analyte concentration with greater precision. Despite the broader resonance peak of the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR, its higher sensitivity, FOM, and resulting improved resolution make it a more effective and precise approach for high-performance sensing applications.

The binding affinity constant can be analyzed to determine the extent of interaction between methylene blue and the surface of various LRSPR configurations. This constant is typically determined through isotherm modeling, which estimates the strength of these interactions. To evaluate the binding affinity of methylene blue with the sensing layer, calibration curves were fitted using the Sips isotherm model (Eq. 1). The Sips isotherm model combines the characteristics of both the Freundlich and Langmuir isotherm models and is expressed as follows [9,39,40]:
(1)
∆ θ =
∆
θ
max
(
K
s
C )
n
1 +
(
K
s
C )
n

Here, ∆θ max represents the maximum SPR shift or the SPR shift at saturation, while C denotes the concentration of the methylene blue. The parameter n is the heterogeneity index, and KS represents the affinity constants in this model. The plots, fitted using the Sips model via the OriginPro fitting tool, are displayed in Fig. 6.

As shown in Fig. 6, the Sips model provides the best fit for the experimental data, with R2 values of 0.984 and 0.848 for the symmetric and anti-symmetric LRSPR configurations using the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer, respectively. The fitted data reveals that the affinity constant (Ks) for the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR is significantly higher than that of the bare Au LRSPR, with values of 1.642 × 104 M−1 and 3.194 × 103 M−1, respectively. This increase in binding affinity is attributed to the functional groups in the AgNP-NCQD-PVA sensing layer, which enhance the attraction and accumulation of methylene blue within the penetration depth region, in contrast to the bare Au layer, which lacks such chemical interaction aids.

Table 2 presents a performance comparison between bare Au LRSPR, AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR, and other reported techniques. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR technique stands out for its broad detection range with two distinct detection slopes. This is due to the dual role of AgNP-NCQD-PVA, which acts as an optical enhancer—boosted by LRSPR's high penetration depth—and as an analyte-interacting layer. This combination enables the sensor to effectively detect a wide range of analyte concentrations, from trace to higher levels, ensuring both sensitivity and practical applicability in real-world scenarios. While its calculated LOD (2.82 ppm) is not as low as some detection methods, it offers better sensitivity and a higher binding affinity, ensuring reliable detection and broader applicability in real-world scenarios, particularly in environmental and industrial contexts. The higher LOD is due to the functional groups in the AgNP-NCQD-PVA sensing material, such as amine and hydroxyl groups, which are not sufficiently strong to interact effectively with methylene blue at lower concentrations [21]. This limited interaction prevents the sensor from achieving a lower LOD, as the material is unable to detect methylene blue at concentrations below a certain threshold. Nonetheless, the sensor’s overall performance remains robust for higher concentration detection.

This study successfully demonstrates the development of an innovative symmetric long-range surface plasmon resonance (LRSPR) technique, significantly advancing photonic sensor technology. By incorporating the AgNP-NCQD-PVA layer as a potential dielectric buffer layer, the sensitivity of symmetric LRSPR for methylene blue detection is substantially enhanced. The AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR showcases exceptional sensitivity with two distinct slopes: 4.2473 nm/ppm for the range of 0.5–5 ppm (R²=0.9231) and 0.5279 nm/ppm for the range of 5–50 ppm (R²=0.9629), surpassing the performance of anti-symmetric LRSPR, which has a more limited detection range of 0.5–5 ppm. Additionally, the AgNP-NCQD-PVA LRSPR provides superior resolution, and a lower detection limit of 0.3 ppm, along with higher binding affinity (1.642 × 104 M−1). The findings show that the AgNP-NCQD-PVA symmetric LRSPR configuration improves sensor performance, particularly in terms of accuracy, reliability, and sensitivity. These enhancements make it suitable for applications such as environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnostics, and chemical analysis, with the potential for further development in photonic sensing technologies.
The author acknowledges the Research University Grant (GUP), grant number GUP-2023-043, funded by the Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Malaysia.

Apsari Retna: Methodology. Khushaini Muhammad Asif Ahmad: Methodology, Investigation. Abdullah Fairuz: Supervision, Resources. Mohd Hafiz Abu Bakar: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Ahmad Ashrif A. Bakar: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mohd Daniyal Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim: Methodology, Investigation. Mobarak Nadhratun Naiim: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Azeman Nur Hidayah: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Othman Muhammad Qayyum: Investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of Generative AI and AI-assisted technologies in the writing process
ce:section.ce:para: While preparing this work, the author used ChatGPT to improve language and readability, with caution. After using this tool/service, the author reviewed and edited the content as needed and took full responsibility for the content of the publication.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Photonics Laboratory of the Department of Electrical, Electronic & Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering and Built Environment, Faculty of Science and Technology, and the Centre for Research and Instrument Management (CRIM), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM) for all amenities provided. Also, this work was supported by Tenaga Nasional Berhad (TNB) and UNITEN through the BOLD Refresh Postdoctoral Fellowships under Grant J510050002-IC-6 BOLDREFRESH2025-Centre of Excellence.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.sna.2025.116249.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Mohd Hafiz Abu Bakar received his B.Sc. (Hons) degree in Chemistry in 2018 and his Ph.D. specializing in chemical sensors in 2022, both from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia. He is currently a Postdoctoral Researcher at the Institute of Power Engineering, Universiti Tenaga Nasional Malaysia. His current research project focuses on the functionalization of polymers and organic substances as active materials for optical sensor applications in water and oil. Additionally, he is exploring surface plasmon resonance (SPR) sensors specifically for oil-based, water, and gas applications.

Muhammad Qayyum Othman a degree in Chemistry from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia. He is currently a master's student at the Photonic Technology Lab at the same university, where he is conducting research on photonic sensors, specifically focusing on developing plasmonic sensors to detect contaminants in edible oils, including palm oil, using advanced photonic technology

Nur Hidayah Azeman. (Member, IEEE) received the B.Sc. degree in resource chemistry from Universiti Malaysia Sarawak, in 2009, the master’s degree in environment and the Ph.D. degree in smart sensing materials from Universiti Putra Malaysia, in 2012 and 2017, respectively. She is currently a Postdoctoral Researcher with Institute of Microengineering and Nanoelectronics, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia. Her project focusing on the designing and synthesizing the sensing materials for the optical sensors’ application. Her research interests include functional materials, chemical sensors, optical sensors, and its applications. In 2020, she has been awarded a travel grant to the Commonwealth Chemistry Congress by the Royal Society of Chemistry, United Kingdom, and Institut Kimia Malaysia.

Nadhratun Naiim Mobarak received the B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees in chemistry from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia (UKM), Malaysia, in 2008, 2011, and 2015, respectively. She is currently a Senior Lecturer with the Department of Chemical Sciences, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia. Her research interest is in the functional biomaterial, water adsorbent and energy conversion and storage devices.

Wan Mohd Ebtisyam Mustaqim Mohd Daniyal completed his Bachelor of Science degree with a major in Physics at Universiti Putra Malaysia (UPM) and earned a Doctor of Philosophy in Sensor Technology. He is currently a lecturer at Universiti Sains Malaysia, where his ongoing research focuses primarily on optical sensors for detecting metal ions.

Muhammad Asif Ahmad Khushaini holds a doctoral degree from the National University of Malaysia (UKM) and earned both his bachelor’s and master’s degrees in physics from Universiti Teknologi MARA (UiTM). Currently serving as a lecturer in Physics at UKM, he specializes in plasmonic and optical technologies, conducting research that aims to push the boundaries of innovation in these critical areas.

Retna Apsari is a distinguished professor in the Faculty of Sciences and Technology, Universitas Airlangga, Surabaya, Indonesia, specializing in bio-optics and laser applications. With a strong commitment to advancing research in her field, she explores a wide range of topics, including bio-optics, fiber optic sensors, material photonics, and photodynamic therapy. Her work aims to bridge the gap between theoretical knowledge and practical applications, contributing to innovative solutions in optical technologies.

Fairuz Abdullah (Senior Member, IEEE) completed his B.Eng. in electronics at Universiti Tenaga Nasional (UNITEN), Malaysia, in 2001. Following that, he earned an M.Sc. in network and communication engineering from Universiti Putra Malaysia in 2004 and a Ph.D. in fiber laser sensors from UNITEN in 2012. Currently serving as an Associate Professor in the Department of Electrical and Electronics Engineering at the College of Engineering and Institute of Power Engineering in UNITEN, his research focuses on optical communications, fiber lasers, optical amplifiers, and fiber optic sensors. Additionally, Fairuz Abdullah actively participates in the IEEE Photonic Society.

Ahmad Ashrif A. Bakar (Senior Member, IEEE) received his bachelor’s degree in Electrical and Electronics Engineering (Universiti Tenaga Nasional) in 2002, Master’s degree in Communications and Network System Engineering (Universiti Putra Malaysia) in 2004, and Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering (University of Queensland, Australia) in 2010. He is actively involved in The Institution of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE Member since 2002 & Senior Member since 2012), Board of Engineers Malaysia (Professional Engineer since 2013), and The Optical Society of America (Member since 2014 & Senior member of OSA since 2020). He devotes his research work to optical sensors in biochemical, biomedical instrumentations, water, and environment applications, specifically in optical techniques of surface plasmon resonance, optical sensing material, and optical feedback interferometry. He is also actively involved in engineering education. He is author and co-author of more than 116 research articles with 4 patents and 9 product developments. He was granted with 55 research grants worth of RM5 Million. He has wide experience in supervising research students; 22 Ph.Ds., 8 Masters, and 26th bachelor’s degree projects. In terms of recognitions, he obtained a professional excellence service award in 2014, being rewarded with several best paper awards in optical conferences and medal awards in research and innovations competitions, etc. Besides that, he was also being appointed as a consultant to assist industries in developing their product development. Furthermore, he is also actively involved in engineering education initiatives. To name a few; promoting engineering subjects and career development to secondary schools, managing STEM activities to nurture engineering subjects, and being one of the board members in school.
